EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high shear environment ahead of an approaching cold front and strong upper dynamics set the stage for severe weather across Southeast GA and Southern SC during the afternoon. A few supercells and a line of thunderstorms with small bows and breaks swept through the region to the west of Interstate 95. Record high temperatures occurred preceding the front which allowed for enough instability to develop in the mid to late afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service damage survey team confirmed that an EF-1 tornado touched down near Islandton, South Carolina. The tornado initially touched down along Jamison Road and traveled east/northeast before lifting along Highway 63. The worst damage occurred along Forks Road and Jamison Road where the tornado damaged or destroyed several structures. At least 3 mobile homes sustained roof damage and at least 30-35 trees were either completely uprooted or snapped. The tornado also snapped several power poles. The most significant damage occurred around 591 Jamison Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beryl developed as a Subtropical Storm over the Atlantic Ocean well east of the South Coastal South Carolina area. The cyclone eventually became a Tropical Storm and slowly moved to the southwest and finally made landfall along the northeast Florida coast. The system then weakened to a Tropical Depression and meandered about before slowly moving back to the northeast across coastal portions of Georgia and South Carolina. The system produced tropical storm force winds, rip currents, and areas of heavy rainfall across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree down on Broomsedge Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was positioned over the southeastern United States throughout the day. A shortwave disturbance combined with isentropic lift and deep layered moisture set up a favorable environment for heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beryl developed as a Subtropical Storm over the Atlantic Ocean well east of the South Coastal South Carolina area. The cyclone eventually became a Tropical Storm and slowly moved to the southwest and finally made landfall along the northeast Florida coast. The system then weakened to a Tropical Depression and meandered about before slowly moving back to the northeast across coastal portions of Georgia and South Carolina. The system produced tropical storm force winds, rip currents, and areas of heavy rainfall across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree down on Diodia Court.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beryl developed as a Subtropical Storm over the Atlantic Ocean well east of the South Coastal South Carolina area. The cyclone eventually became a Tropical Storm and slowly moved to the southwest and finally made landfall along the northeast Florida coast. The system then weakened to a Tropical Depression and meandered about before slowly moving back to the northeast across coastal portions of Georgia and South Carolina. The system produced tropical storm force winds, rip currents, and areas of heavy rainfall across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on a car near Gavins Road and Route 15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong blocking ridge over the Atlantic Ocean and an upper level trough extending from the Ohio Valley into the Southern Plains placed the region within a broad and deep layer cyclonic flow regime. At the surface, a very slow moving cold front extended from the upstate of South Carolina, across east-central Georgia and the Florida Panhandle. Frequent boundary interactions at the surface aided in the development of numerous showers and thunderstorms across the area during the mid-late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported penny size hail on Blue Gill Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beryl developed as a Subtropical Storm over the Atlantic Ocean well east of the South Coastal South Carolina area. The cyclone eventually became a Tropical Storm and slowly moved to the southwest and finally made landfall along the northeast Florida coast. The system then weakened to a Tropical Depression and meandered about before slowly moving back to the northeast across coastal portions of Georgia and South Carolina. The system produced tropical storm force winds, rip currents, and areas of heavy rainfall across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flooded into a residence at 129 Bowman Street from heavy rainfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Darlington County Emergency Manager conducted a storm survey. A downburst with winds to 85 mph lifted and destroyed the roof of a detached two car garage. The side wall and part of the front wall of this structure collapsed. The debris was blown onto adjacent property to include three 2x4s which pierced the outer wall of a mobile home. Three large cedar trees were blown down. A pecan orchard sustained considerable tree damage. The damage occurred along 0.2 miles of Christmas Tree Road. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The cap held things in check until mid to late afternoon, then pulse storms developed due to a short wave aloft. This was followed by numerous outflows interacting with each other, upstream convection and the sea breeze which generated multicellular clusters as the afternoon progressed. With DCAPES in some areas over 1000 j/kg, there were numerous reports of trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along and behind a cold front aligned roughly along the Grand Strand. Hail up to the size of golf balls was reported. Hail accumulated to a significant depth in several locations. In the aftermath of the storms, some areas fogged in as the low levels cooled rapidly where the hail remained on the ground for some length of time. Murrells Inlet, South Carolina was especially hard hit as slick roads and reduced visibility caused numerous accidents. Highway 17 was closed for a time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of quarters was reported on Salters Road near the intersection with Highway 17 Business.

With DCAPES in some areas over 1000 j/kg, there were numerous reports of trees down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down near the intersection of Highway 164 and Rodd Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across the area during the late afternoon and evening hours. The atmosphere was highly unstable and low level lapse rates were steep. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of quarters was reported at the intersection of Highway 17 and Highway 707.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Short wave moving in from NW, surface wave inland and plentiful mesoscale boundaries yet again. Storms developed into clusters and pushed off to the east and southeast. DCAPES not as high as recent days, but still supportive of strong wet microbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SC Highway Patrol reports a tree down and in the roadway on Haven Road near Horseford Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along and behind a cold front aligned roughly along the Grand Strand. Hail up to the size of golf balls was reported. Hail accumulated to a significant depth in several locations. In the aftermath of the storms, some areas fogged in as the low levels cooled rapidly where the hail remained on the ground for some length of time. Murrells Inlet, South Carolina was especially hard hit as slick roads and reduced visibility caused numerous accidents. Highway 17 was closed for a time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of quarters was reported near the intersection of Dongola Highway and Kates Bay Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak mid-level sheared vorticity maxima dropped south across the area during the later afternoon and into the evening. Clusters of thunderstorms developed in response to this feature. An outflow boundary along the coast pushed inland and likely enhanced some of the thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downburst caused wind damage in Andrews, South Carolina. The path length was just over a mile and a quarter, from Conifer Street and North Rosemary Avenue to South Farr Avenue and South Beech Avenue to Cedar Avenue and West Hemlock Street. Eight large trees were blown down and blocking roadways. Fallen trees knocked down power lines. One large tree fell on a car, trapping three people inside, but there were no injuries. The corner of a roof was ripped off a house, a variety store and two out buildings sustained minor roof damage. Power was reportedly off in the city of Andrews.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong blocking ridge over the Atlantic Ocean and an upper level trough extending from the Ohio Valley into the Southern Plains placed the region within a broad and deep layer cyclonic flow regime. At the surface, a very slow moving cold front extended from the upstate of South Carolina, across east-central Georgia and the Florida Panhandle. Frequent boundary interactions at the surface aided in the development of numerous showers and thunderstorms across the area during the mid-late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer reported penny size hail near Ridgeland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated along the Piedmont trough during the early afternoon and quickly became severe as they organized and generated a cold pool. This cold pool bowed out ahead of the main line of storms through the late afternoon and early evening. Incredible instability was present with CAPE values in excess of 6000 J/kg combined with steep lapse rates to produce widespread severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down near the intersection of Highway 512 and Highway 513.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service damage survey team evaluated damage that occurred with a severe thunderstorm that moved through portions of central Jasper County Monday afternoon.  The team found considerable damage to trees south of Ridgeland with a damage pattern consistent with a tornado.||The tornado touched down just west of Jasper Road between the communities of Okatie and Switzerland and moved rapidly east-northeast before lifting near the intersection of South Carolina Highway 462 and Snake road. The worst damage occurred between Jasper Road and Riley Field Road where the team estimated between 300 and 500 medium to large-size trees were either snapped or uprooted.  Some debarking of trees was also noted.  This degree of|tree damage was consistent with an ef-1 tornado on the enhanced Fujita scale with winds estimated to be between 85 and 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low shifted over the central Appalachians with a negatively tilted trough extending into southeast South Carolina. A strong cold front associated with this feature enhanced the pressure gradient over the local area during the morning and afternoon hours. Strong cold air advection along the front produced 30 to 40 mph wind gusts as low level winds mixed down to the surface.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High shear characterized with 70 knots of bulk shear along with low surface based cape set up a high shear, low cape environment ahead of the trough over the southeastern United States.  A distinct line of thunderstorms developed along a surface trough and became strong as the trough swept through the Southeast. Although there was a tornado watch issued for the entire forecast area, all reported damage was contributed to straight-line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along Tillman Road near the Wagon Branch Loop. Large tree limbs were also blown down near the intersection of West Main Street and Rail Road Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low over the deep south opened up and rapidly crossed the western Carolinas during the evening hours of the 11th. A dry slot associated with the low overspread the region, bringing most of the rain and shower activity to an end during the afternoon hours. However, a small line of thunderstorms developed along the surface cold front over the western Upstate. The southern-most cell in the line began to exhibit mini-supercell characteristics as it moved across the upstate and into the southern foothills of North Carolina. While the storm only produced some hail over the Upstate, it spawned three tornadoes over western North Carolina.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High shear characterized with 70 knots of bulk shear along with low surface based cape set up a high shear, low cape environment ahead of the trough over the southeastern United States.  A distinct line of thunderstorms developed along a surface trough and became strong as the trough swept through the Southeast. Although there was a tornado watch issued for the entire forecast area, all reported damage was contributed to straight-line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Carolina Highway Patrol reported a tree down near the intersection of Highway 170 and Highway 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderately unstable airmass was lifted by a cold front approaching from the north and by a seabreeze advancing inland. Bulk shear values were enhanced by the passage of a mid-level wind speed maximum during the early evening hours. Thunderstorms began as discrete single cells and multi-cells during the late afternoon before transitioning into a squall line as individual cold pools merged during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of quarters was reported. The report was relayed by a media outlet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep closed low approaching from the west resulted in fairly cold temps aloft. Unstable airmass developed ahead of a slow-moving cold front that eventually pushed offshore after 3 am. First bout of convection occurred late morning through early evening. Another round of strong/severe storms erupted along the front itself between 8p and 1a...mainly hail-producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jasper County 911 call center reported 1 tree down along Interstate 95 1 mile southwest of Grahamville.

Thunderstorms produced scattered wind damage and multiple instances of large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a day where temperatures rose to record levels, including 107 degrees at the Greenville-Spartanburg International Airport, numerous severe storms developed over western South Carolina during the afternoon and evening hours. The wind shear was fairly weak, but there was considerable instability. This allowed some of the storms to organize into small bowing clusters, though the dominant mode was pulse severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell at the intersection of Armenia Rd and highway 321.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in the upper 90s/lower 100s and dewpoints in the mid 70s/around 80 created tremendous instability featuring SBCapes 6000-8000 j/kg, downdraft capes 1500-2000 j/kg and hail capes 1500-2500 j/kg. Also of note: very warm surface-700 mb temperatures and 500 mb tempuratures -10 to -12C generates 700-500 mb lapse rates around 9 c/km. Thunderstorms fired along an inland surface trough axis; multicell thunderstorms then generated cold pools which drove severe convection through virtually the entire forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Oppressive heat continued the first day of July over the Upstate, with Greenville-Spartanburg International Airport hitting an all-time record high temperature of 107 degrees. However, low dewpoints kept the heat index just below 110. The Greenwood ASOS had a max temp of 107 and a heat index of at least 110. At Rock Hill the high was 104, but the heat index topped out at 112 degrees as dewpoints were higher in the lower elevations of the Catawba River valley. Widespread thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, bringing a few days of relief from the heat.

An oppressively hot and humid airmass that spent several days building west of the Appalachians finally made it east of the mountains, bringing very hot conditions to upstate South Carolina. The high temperature at Greenville-Spartanburg International Airport hit 105 degrees on the 29th and 103 degrees on the 30th, both records for the day. The heat index hit 106 degrees. The high temperature at Anderson hit 105 degrees on both the 29th and 30th with a heat index up to 107 degrees. Excessive heat affected areas south of Interstate 85. The Greenwood ASOS hit 106 degrees both days, with a heat index of 110 degrees on the 30th. The heat index also hit 110 at the Rock Hill ASOS. An elderly woman died on the 30th from heat-related complications in an Easley nursing home after the air conditioning quit working. Even hotter conditions extended through July 1st, before thunderstorms brought somewhat cooler conditions.

Scattered thunderstorms moved into upstate South Carolina from western North Carolina along a southward moving cold front. Most of the storms remained below severe limits, though a few caused a little wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and power line were blown down across Holly Springs School Rd about 6 miles north northwest of Pickens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable airmass and weak northwest flow supported the development of scattered thunderstorms over western South Carolina. A few of the storms caused wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell in the Homeland Park community.

Despite weak upper flow, a very unstable airmass supported the development of scattered thunderstorms over the region. Most of the storms developed along the Blue Ridge with subsequent outflow boundaries supporting additional convection across the foothills and Piedmont. In many cases damaging outflow winds were caused by rather weak storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs blown down around Table Rock State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An oppressively hot and humid airmass that spent several days building west of the Appalachians finally made it east of the mountains, bringing very hot conditions to upstate South Carolina. The high temperature at Greenville-Spartanburg International Airport hit 105 degrees on the 29th and 103 degrees on the 30th, both records for the day. The heat index hit 106 degrees. The high temperature at Anderson hit 105 degrees on both the 29th and 30th with a heat index up to 107 degrees. Excessive heat affected areas south of Interstate 85. The Greenwood ASOS hit 106 degrees both days, with a heat index of 110 degrees on the 30th. The heat index also hit 110 at the Rock Hill ASOS. An elderly woman died on the 30th from heat-related complications in an Easley nursing home after the air conditioning quit working. Even hotter conditions extended through July 1st, before thunderstorms brought somewhat cooler conditions.

Temperatures in the upper 90s/lower 100s and dewpoints in the mid 70s/around 80 created tremendous instability featuring SBCapes 6000-8000 j/kg, downdraft capes 1500-2000 j/kg and hail capes 1500-2500 j/kg. Also of note: very warm surface-700 mb temperatures and 500 mb tempuratures -10 to -12C generates 700-500 mb lapse rates around 9 c/km. Thunderstorms fired along an inland surface trough axis; multicell thunderstorms then generated cold pools which drove severe convection through virtually the entire forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reported one power line down in Martin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Sandy weakened to a tropical storm while shifting north from the Bahamas to the Southeastern United States. While traveling north, the edge of Sandy skirted along the South Carolina coast.  The pressure gradient field associated with Sandy was large, which eventually produced tropical storm force winds over elevated bridges in Charleston County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor on the US-17 Ravenel Bridge reported a wind gust of 40 mph. Height of sensor is estimated at 250 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level shortwave trough moved by to the northwest and north of the area. Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front late in the day. The environment was characterized by marginal instability and moderate shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of quarters was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated along the Piedmont trough during the early afternoon and quickly became severe as they organized and generated a cold pool. This cold pool bowed out ahead of the main line of storms through the late afternoon and early evening. Incredible instability was present with CAPE values in excess of 6000 J/kg combined with steep lapse rates to produce widespread severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees were reported down across power lines along Prevatte Road. Power lines were reported down on Martin Street. A large tree was also reported down across Old Nichols Highway.

This cold pool bowed out ahead of the main line of storms through the late afternoon and early evening. The heat index went over 110 degrees beginning the afternoon of June 29th, continuing through the next day. The highest heat index noted was near 120 degrees. There were no reports of heat related illnesses or fatalities.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down along Coxe Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough was in place across the eastern United States with an inland surface trough in the lee of the Appalachian Mountains. Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed near the surface trough and affected much of Georgia and South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees down on Ironhorse Way and Holiness Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of isolated severe thunderstorms produced some 1 inch hail and wind damage in the upper Pee Dee region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to an inch in diameter was reported by a CO-OP observer.  Tree limbs were also down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along multiple surface boundary interactions. A cold pool aloft allowed thunderstorms to intensify and several storms did produce large hail. There were also reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of golf balls was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of storms moved through the Midlands and produced large hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kangaroo Express Gas Station at Main and Whaley streets reported the intersection under 2 to 4 feet of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved through the CSRA and Midlands producing wind damage, large hail, and two book end tornadoes.  Several homes were severely damaged and there were widespread trees and powerlines down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported several trees down along Hwy 321 from North to Woodford.

This cold pool bowed out ahead of the main line of storms through the late afternoon and early evening. Incredible instability was present with CAPE values in excess of 6000 J/kg combined with steep lapse rates to produce widespread severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One-inch hail was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along and behind a cold front aligned roughly along the Grand Strand. Hail up to the size of golf balls was reported. Hail accumulated to a significant depth in several locations. In the aftermath of the storms, some areas fogged in as the low levels cooled rapidly where the hail remained on the ground for some length of time. Murrells Inlet, South Carolina was especially hard hit as slick roads and reduced visibility caused numerous accidents. Highway 17 was closed for a time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of quarters was reported at the Tanger Outlet Mall on Highway 501.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over upstate South Carolina during the evening hours. A few of the storms caused wind damage and produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported in the Fort Mill area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Torrential heavy rainfall developed in the late morning hours within the broad circulation and deep tropical moisture around Tropical Cyclone Isaac. The heavy rainfall producing thunderstorms produced widespread flash flooding in and around Downtown Charleston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Charleston Emergency Management reported twenty separate stranded vehicles in and around Downtown Charleston that police and fire department crews are responding to.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of storms moved through the Midlands and produced large hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COCORAHS observer reported quarter size hail near the Sweetwater Golf Club.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms in Richland County produced wind damage to trees and some localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Police reported trees down on Hutto Ct.

This cold pool bowed out ahead of the main line of storms through the late afternoon and early evening. Incredible instability was present with CAPE values in excess of 6000 J/kg combined with steep lapse rates to produce widespread severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A metal roof was reportedly blown off a building on East Broadway Street.

The wind shear was fairly weak, but there was considerable instability. This allowed some of the storms to organize into small bowing clusters, though the dominant mode was pulse severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down along the highway 14 corridor in Five Forks and the north side of Simpsonville. Shingle damage to homes was also reported in the five Forks area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated pulse-type thunderstorms developed over western North Carolina during the afternoon hours. A couple of the storms produced small areas of damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large tree limbs were blown down at Musgrove Mill State Historic Site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms in the east Midlands produced some large hail and wind damage in eastern Sumter, southern Lee, and northern Clarendon counties.  Main areas impacted were the Mayesville, Shiloh, and Turbeville areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported trees down in Irmo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered, pulse-type thunderstorms developed over upstate Sorth Carolina during the afternoon and evening hours. With a very unstable airmass in place over the region, several of the storms produced large hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down between Greenwood and Ninety Six.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced large hail and wind damage in the Midlands and Pee Dee regions.  Damage was limited mainly to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public sent report of 1.5 inch hail and picture to WIS TV.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced large hail and wind damage in the Midlands and Pee Dee regions.  Damage was limited mainly to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public, from NWS storm survey interview, reported golf ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms in the Midlands area produced large hail and some wind damage taking down some trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Puplic reported quarter size hail along Boone Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of Tropical Storm Beryl produced an EF1 tornado in the Holly Hill area taking down trees, corn crops, and doing minor damage to a home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway Patrol reported trees down near Cordova and Cherry Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms dropped 4 to 6 inches of rain over portions of Lancaster and Chesterfield counties flooding many roads and low lying areas.  Several roads were closed and one vehicle got trapped in flooded waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported a car trapped in flood water on Cimmeron road off Rocky River road.  Dixie School road also closed due flooded waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures in the upper 90s/lower 100s and dewpoints in the mid 70s/around 80 created tremendous instability featuring SBCapes 6000-8000 j/kg, downdraft capes 1500-2000 j/kg and hail capes 1500-2500 j/kg. Also of note: very warm surface-700 mb temperatures and 500 mb tempuratures -10 to -12C generates 700-500 mb lapse rates around 9 c/km. Thunderstorms fired along an inland surface trough axis; multicell thunderstorms then generated cold pools which drove severe convection through virtually the entire forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager relayed reports of 33 trees and 17 power lines down in and around Walterboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Within a warm sector south and southeast of a surface low pressure system and an associated cold front, thunderstorms fired along pre-frontal mesoscale boundaries early in the afternoon and advanced east across the forecast area through the evening. A combination of parameters supported multi-cell convection and a few severe thunderstorms damaging wind gusts and quarter sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Carolina Highway Patrol reported a tree down at US 601 South and Clifton Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across Upstate South Carolina during the evening hours as an upper low crossed the region. The storms produced a little large hail and spotty reports of wind damage.
Widely scattered storms produced some large hail and strong winds that took down trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported trees down at Hickory Grove Road and Hollywood Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a day where temperatures rose to record levels, including 107 degrees at the Greenville-Spartanburg International Airport, numerous severe storms developed over western South Carolina during the afternoon and evening hours. The wind shear was fairly weak, but there was considerable instability. This allowed some of the storms to organize into small bowing clusters, though the dominant mode was pulse severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down in the western part of the county, from the Hickory Grove area to McConnells.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered storms produced some large hail and strong winds that took down trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DOT reported trees down along SC145, US1, and Crossroads Church Hwy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms formed along the seabreeze and outflow boundaries over southeast South Carolina. The collapsing storms produced several reports of downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch Center reported a car that hit a downed tree down on Jefferies Blvd in Walterboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms produced mainly wind damage and some hail over the Midlads of South Carolina taking down trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Columbia Metropolitan Airport recorded 2.16 inches of rain in an hour. Most of the rain fell between 820 PM AND 920 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few isolated thunderstorms developed over Edgefield, McCormick, and Aiken counties and produced penny size hail.
Short waves aloft, mesoscale boundaries (such as sea breeze, outflows and differential heating) and DCAPES of 1000-1500 j/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines down on 2nd Texas Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front, oriented east to west across the area, became a focus for convection. Thunderstorms developed to the west of the area and then propagated along the front and across the area during the evening. A mid-level shortwave trough helped to enhance the severity of the thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was partially lifted off a mobile home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the mountains ahead of the cold front. The storms then moved southeast, into upstate South Carolina. The storms produced quite a bit of hail, some large, over the central part of the upstate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell near the intersection of I-85 and White Horse Rd. A spotter also reported penny size hail at the intersection of I-85 and highway 276.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple supercell thunderstorms developed along a stationary front over upstate South Carolina. The storms produced several reports of large hail and a little straight line wind damage as they slowly moved east, just north of the surface front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported about a mile southeast of Woodruff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over the eastern Upstate of South Carolina during the afternoon hours ahead of a strong upper low. Large hail and localized wind damage was reported with a few of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down power lines on Burris Rd about 4 miles south of Sharon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along multiple surface boundary interactions. A cold pool aloft allowed thunderstorms to intensify and several storms did produce large hail. There were also reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of quarters was reported. The hail persisted for about 30 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High instability and steep lapse rates developed during the afternoon in response to strong heating. A potent upper low moved overhead, creating a cold pool of minus 16 degrees Celsius at 500 mb.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of quarters was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated along the Piedmont trough during the early afternoon and quickly became severe as they organized and generated a cold pool. This cold pool bowed out ahead of the main line of storms through the late afternoon and early evening. Incredible instability was present with CAPE values in excess of 6000 J/kg combined with steep lapse rates to produce widespread severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches reported broken and down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced damaging winds and some hail around the Midlands.  Most wind damage was from trees and powerlines going down.  Locally heavy rains also caused flash flooing in Downtown Columbia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Through 8pm 2.26 inches of rain fell at Owens Field... 1.85 inches of the total fell in a 27 minute period from 653pm to 720pm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway Patrol reported trees in the road at Beaver Dam Creek Road and Bush River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Many severe thunderstorms developed over the Midlands and CSRA producing large hail and wind damage.  Hail sizes were mostly golf ball to tennis ball size.  The SC Insurance News Service reported home and auto claims of around 7 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter size hail on 3 CS Road.
